title: What Is Grip Strength Testing? Why It Matters as a Health Biomarker
description: Grip strength testing measures hand and forearm force as an indicator of overall muscle strength, frailty, and mortality risk. This guide covers protocols, normative values, and clinical applications.
author: Able Care Clinical Team

blocks.question: What is grip strength testing?
blocks.directAnswer: Grip strength testing measures the maximum isometric force of the hand and forearm using a dynamometer. It is one of the most validated biomarkers in clinical medicine, with over 50 years of research linking low grip strength to increased mortality, cardiovascular disease, frailty, and disability. Every 5 kg decrease in grip strength is associated with a 17% increase in all-cause mortality risk (Leong et al., Lancet, 2015).

tldrBullets.text: Grip strength is recognized as a "vital sign" for aging populations by the World Health Organization.
Normal values: approximately 30-55 kg for men and 20-35 kg for women, depending on age.
Below 27 kg (men) or 16 kg (women) meets the EWGSOP2 threshold for probable sarcopenia.
Able Assess uses the GripAble digital dynamometer with Bluetooth data capture for objective, standardized measurement.

sources.title: Leong DP et al. Prognostic value of grip strength. The Lancet.
Cruz-Jentoft AJ et al. Sarcopenia: revised European consensus (EWGSOP2). Age and Ageing.
Roberts HC et al. A review of the measurement of grip strength in clinical and epidemiological studies. Age and Ageing.

blocks.reviewedBy: Prof James Mitchell, Professor of Rehabilitation Medicine

blocks.body:
Why does grip strength matter?

Grip strength is one of the simplest, cheapest, and most predictive clinical measurements available. The Prospective Urban Rural Epidemiology (PURE) study, which followed 139,691 adults across 17 countries over four years, found that grip strength was a stronger predictor of all-cause mortality than systolic blood pressure (Leong et al., Lancet, 2015).

This is because grip strength is not just a measure of hand force. It reflects overall skeletal muscle mass and function, nutritional status, and biological aging. Low grip strength is an early warning sign of:

  • Sarcopenia — age-related loss of muscle mass and function
  • Frailty — a state of increased vulnerability to adverse health outcomes
  • Cardiovascular disease — each 5 kg decrease in grip strength is associated with a 17% increase in cardiovascular mortality
  • Respiratory disease — grip strength predicts COPD exacerbation frequency and pneumonia outcomes
  • Post-surgical complications — pre-operative grip strength predicts length of stay and 30-day readmission

How is grip strength measured?

The standard protocol involves three maximal-effort squeezes with the dominant hand, using a calibrated dynamometer. The highest of three readings is typically recorded. Testing should be performed with the patient seated, elbow at 90 degrees, and forearm in neutral rotation.

Traditional dynamometers (Jamar, Saehan, Baseline) are hydraulic or mechanical devices that display force on an analogue dial. They require manual recording, are prone to calibration drift, and offer no data export.

Digital dynamometers (GripAble, Biometrics E-Link) capture force data electronically, enabling Bluetooth transfer to tablets or computers. The GripAble sensor used in Able Assess records peak force, time-to-peak, force curve shape, and fatigue metrics, all stored automatically against the patient record.

Normal grip strength by age and sex

Normative values vary by age, sex, hand dominance, and population. The following reference ranges are derived from the Southampton study (Roberts et al., 2011) and EWGSOP2 consensus (Cruz-Jentoft et al., 2019):

Men: Ages 60-69: 36-51 kg. Ages 70-79: 30-45 kg. Ages 80+: 24-38 kg. Below 27 kg meets the EWGSOP2 threshold for probable sarcopenia.

Women: Ages 60-69: 22-33 kg. Ages 70-79: 18-28 kg. Ages 80+: 14-22 kg. Below 16 kg meets the EWGSOP2 threshold for probable sarcopenia.

Able Assess automatically compares each measurement against age- and sex-stratified normative data and assigns a risk band (within normal range, below normal, or significantly below normal).

Clinical applications of grip strength

Grip strength testing is used across a widening range of clinical contexts:

  • Falls risk assessment — grip strength is one of four metrics in the Able Assess protocol, alongside gait speed, sit-to-stand, and TUG
  • Sarcopenia screening — the EWGSOP2 algorithm uses grip strength as the first-line screening test for probable sarcopenia
  • Pre-surgical risk stratification — low grip strength predicts post-operative complications across orthopedic, cardiac, and abdominal surgery
  • Oncology — grip strength predicts treatment tolerance and quality of life in cancer patients receiving chemotherapy
  • GLP-1 medication monitoring — semaglutide and tirzepatide cause lean mass loss alongside fat loss; grip strength monitoring tracks whether muscle function is preserved
  • Clinical trials — grip strength is an accepted endpoint for trials in sarcopenia, frailty, cachexia, and rehabilitation

faqs.question: What is a normal grip strength for a 70-year-old?
faqs.answer: For a 70-year-old man, normal grip strength is approximately 30 to 45 kg. For a 70-year-old woman, normal range is approximately 18 to 28 kg. Values below the EWGSOP2 threshold (27 kg for men, 16 kg for women) warrant further investigation for sarcopenia.

faqs.question: Is grip strength a reliable indicator of overall health?
faqs.answer: Yes. Grip strength is one of the most validated biomarkers in clinical research. The PURE study (139,691 participants, 17 countries) found it was a stronger predictor of cardiovascular mortality than systolic blood pressure. It is recognized by the WHO as a key indicator of healthy aging.

faqs.question: How often should grip strength be tested?
faqs.answer: For general population screening, annual testing is appropriate for adults over 65. In clinical settings (rehabilitation, oncology, post-surgery), more frequent testing (monthly or per visit) tracks response to treatment. For GLP-1 medication monitoring, quarterly testing is recommended to detect lean mass loss early.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a Jamar and a digital dynamometer?
faqs.answer: The Jamar dynamometer is the traditional hydraulic reference standard but requires manual reading, manual recording, and periodic recalibration. Digital dynamometers like GripAble capture data electronically via Bluetooth, record the full force curve (not just peak force), and export data directly to clinical systems. Both produce reliable peak force measurements when used correctly.
